original_abstract:
Here, we propose an all-analog chip combining electronic and light computing (ACCEL). While photonic computing offers the potential for faster and more energy-efficient vision data processing, achieving experimental superiority in deployable systems remains difficult due to complex optical nonlinearities, the heavy power consumption of analog-to-digital converters (ADCs), and sensitivity to system noise.

ACCEL overcomes these barriers by fusing a diffractive optical analog processor with an integrated electronic analog chip. In this architecture, light-induced photocurrents are used directly for calculation without the need for ADCs, enabling an ultra-low computing latency of 72 ns per frame. Experimentally, the system achieves a systemic energy efficiency of 74.8 peta-operations per second per watt and a computing speed of 4.6 peta-operations per second (with over 99% of operations implemented optically). These figures represent improvements of more than three orders of magnitude in efficiency and one order of magnitude in speed compared to state-of-the-art processors.

Through the joint optimization of optoelectronic computing and adaptive training, ACCEL achieves competitive experimental classification accuracies of 85.5% for Fashion-MNIST, 82.0% for 3-class ImageNet, and 92.6% for time-lapse video recognition. Notably, the system demonstrates exceptional robustness in low-light conditions (0.14 fJ μm⁻² per frame). Given its high speed and efficiency, ACCEL is well-suited for a broad range of high-demand applications, including wearable devices, autonomous driving, and industrial inspections.
humanized_abstract:
There have been several proposals for all-analog chips for electronic and light computing. Photonic computing, which could accelerate and greatly reduce power consumption for vision data processing, has shown experiment results that are only a prototype of the systems that will be deployed in future applications, due to difficult-to-overcome nonlinearity problems of light, high power consumption of ADCs and sensitivity to noises.

ACCEL advances the state of the art in the computing performance and energy efficiency of deep learning accelerators in two main ways. First, an integrated diffractive optical analog processor and an electronic analog circuit on a silicon chip is developed. ACCEL carries out calculations using light-induced photocurrents bypassing analog-to-digital conversion (ADC), resulting in an ultra-low computing latency of 72 ns per frame. Experiments demonstrate a systemic energy efficiency of 74.8 peta-operations per second per watt, and a computing speed of 4.6 peta-operations per second with over 99% of operations carried out optically — exceeding the energy efficiency and computing performance of existing computing architectures by over three orders of magnitude, and by one order of magnitude respectively.

ACCEL is an in-situ neuromorphic photonic optoelectronic computing system that uses adaptive training to jointly optimize the performance of the photonic compute elements and the classification accuracy of the machine learning model. Achieving experimental classification accuracies of 85.5% on Fashion-MNIST, 82.0% on 3-class ImageNet, and 92.6% on time-lapse video classification, the system is shown to operate with high robustness under low light conditions (0.14 fJ μm⁻² per frame). The high-speed, energy-efficient operation of the system makes it highly suitable for many high demand applications such as wearable electronics, self-driving cars, and industrial inspections.
